insight: Savior requires strong, easily-accessible prompt defense (like Polywall or Doomed Wall) to execute safely, especially on turn 3, because early-game rushes like Gauss Cannon spam or other quick attackers can kill you before you stabilize.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion of when Savior is viable in different unit sets and what defensive support it needs.

insight: Polywall is the single best support unit for Savior because it provides cheap prompt soak without requiring additional tech investment, unlike Aegis which demands Conduit tech (ccc) that delays other economic purchases.
author: mrguy888
context: Analysis of which defensive units best support Savior strategies.

insight: Perforator is primarily a utility/defensive soak unit, not a main attacker. Buying it as a primary offensive unit effectively costs 6R when accounting for the red production needed, making Tarsier spam vastly more efficient for attack.
author: consensus
context: Detailed analysis of Perforator's role and cost-efficiency compared to Tarsier.

insight: Perforator's delayed build time (2 turns to first attack) effectively costs attackers ~2 damage per turn compared to Tarsier, equivalent to giving the opponent ~4G per turn, making Perforator-based offenses cost approximately 6R per effective attack when fully considered.
author: amalloy
context: Mathematical analysis of why Tarsier dominates as a red attacker despite Perforator's cheaper upfront cost.

insight: In Centrifuge sets, buying Centrifuge to accelerate a single Colossus is inefficient; instead, build all four Colossus on successive turns naturally, or skip Centrifuge entirely if it would disrupt other economic goals.
author: redzonex
context: Discussion of when Centrifuge is worth purchasing versus building Colossus naturally.

insight: Centrifuge is best used in mid-economy pools to accelerate expensive, high-tech investment units (like Odin, Apollo, Vai Mauronax, Lucina) that you would rather not fully tech up for or build economy into, providing a resource-efficient tech jump.
author: redzonex
context: Analysis of Centrifuge's best use cases in mid-game economy builds.

insight: In Savior sets, Player 1 can execute a turn 4 Savior with 3rd Engineer (dd dd dd1 into turn 4 Savior) if the opponent doesn't rush, allowing an economic lead that becomes very difficult for P2 to overcome without mirroring.
author: spiritfryer
context: Discussion of early Savior timing and viability against various P2 responses.

insight: The dd dd dd1 opening (with third Engineer) is risky against P2 dd ddc because it lacks defense; better to go dd dd dd + Engineer to maintain flexibility to react to P2 plays like 2.da (Animus) while still having Savior as an option.
author: p0lari
context: Analysis of Savior opening flexibility and risk management against aggressive P2 responses.

insight: Gauss Charge is efficient for forcing out a Forcefield early in the game (trading 1G to force a Drone→Forcefield block), but becomes bad late when the opponent would have blocked with a Drone anyway, making it a tempo tool rather than a damage tool.
author: .bky_1556
context: Analysis of Gauss Charge's efficiency against Forcefield at different game stages.

insight: Holding Drones indefinitely to avoid buying Forcefield is inefficient (the Drones become 'dead'); instead, buy Forcefields early to defend against real threats, since held Drones provide no value and Forcefields are cheaper long-term.
author: awaclus
context: Discussion of Forcefield purchase timing versus holding Drones for defense.

insight: Two Shiver Yetis against Defense Grid force the opponent to over-defend by 6 damage or lose either absorb or granularity, but this is only worthwhile if you can create an additional inconvenience (like a granularity exploit) the turn after buying them.
author: .bky_1556
context: Analysis of Shiver Yeti's effectiveness as a multi-copy unit against Defense Grid.

insight: In base+Grimbotch as P1, playing natural Animus (rather than Blastforge) is often superior because it commits less: if P2 responds with Blastforge, you get Rhino+Grimbotch+Engineer; if P2 responds with Grimbotch, you can still build Rhino+Grimbotch+Engineer next turn.
author: mqp
context: Analysis of optimal P1 responses to opponent's first Animus in base+Grimbotch.

insight: Antima Comet is strong when there is no soak available for the opponent to block it with, allowing a direct HP reduction; Arka (presumably Arkanishu) can be countered by absorb units like Arbalest.
author: mqp
context: Brief discussion of Antima Comet's mechanics and how to sequence it optimally.

insight: Floating resources (producing more resources than you can spend) is a sign of inefficient building; for example, buying a second Conduit that generates Green you can't spend that turn is worse than buying alternate units like Husk+Tarsier+Engineer that directly apply resources to the game plan.
author: spiritfryer
context: Coaching analysis of replay showing inefficient resource generation and spending.

insight: Grenade Mech with Redeemer is strong enough to prevent Omega Splitter (mostly unsupported) from being viable in a set; the pressure is too high for the opponent to setup and execute Omega.
author: spiritfryer
context: Analysis of whether Omega Splitter can function against strong defensive pressure.

insight: In base+Grimbotch, P1 should avoid natural Animus and instead play Elyot Animus or natural Blastforge; natural Animus falls behind to Grimbotch/Tarsier/Perforator pressure and Tatsu punishes defending positions.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion of viable P1 opening strategies in base+Grimbotch.
